item.title: DNS Server
item.author: user@example.com (Hugo Author)
item.description: The Problem Most networking tutorials treat DNS as a black box: call getaddrinfo(), receive an IP. This project goes the other direction — the server is the resolver. It receives raw DNS queries over UDP, parses binary wire format by hand, looks up records in zone files, and only calls upstream when it can’t answer authoritatively. The engineering challenge is binary protocol implementation: DNS packets have no delimiters, use compressed label encoding, and require byte-order discipline at every field boundary.

item.title: IRC Daemon
item.author: user@example.com (Hugo Author)
item.description: The Problem The naive approach to a multi-client server is one thread per connection: accept a client, spawn a thread, read in a loop. This works up to a few hundred clients, then collapses under thread scheduling overhead and per-thread stack allocation. The alternative is I/O multiplexing: one thread, one poll() call, many file descriptors. The operating system tells you which sockets are ready to read. You service them in sequence.

item.title: LuminaLocal
item.author: user@example.com (Hugo Author)
item.description: The Problem Sending candidate resumes to a cloud LLM API creates two hard constraints that most hiring pipelines ignore: PII leaves your perimeter, and cost scales linearly with batch size. At 500 resumes per hiring cycle, even a compact GPT-4o prompt accumulates thousands of dollars in token spend — before accounting for revision loops. The alternative is local inference. But local models produce unstructured output, hallucinate missing skills, and have no built-in retry logic.

item.title: NCTU Plus API
item.author: user@example.com (Hugo Author)
item.description: The Problem Building an authenticated web platform is straightforward when you own the identity system. NCTU Plus had a harder constraint: authentication must go through NYCU’s OAuth2 provider. The university controls user accounts, session validity, and profile data. Your API is a downstream consumer — you receive an authorization code, exchange it for a profile, and then issue your own session tokens. This creates a three-party trust chain: browser → NYCU OAuth → your API.

item.title: RTX 5090 on Ubuntu Server: Bypassing Common Traps
item.author: user@example.com (Hugo Author)
item.description: RTX 5090 on Ubuntu Server: Bypassing Common Traps Prerequisites Use Rufus to create a bootable USB from the Ubuntu Server ISO image. https://rufus.ie/ https://ubuntu.com/download/server 8g usb Install Ubuntu Select all disks except the one that will be used as the boot device. RAID Setup Partitioning On each drive: Allocate 2 TB as unformatted Leave the remaining space as unformatted as well RAID 10 (ext4) mounted at / Choose Software RAID Select all 2 TB unformatted partitions Create a RAID 10 array Format as ext4 Mount point: / RAID 10 (btrfs, compressed) mounted at /service Choose Software RAID Select the remaining unformatted space Create a RAID 10 array Format as btrfs Enable compression Mount point: /service Post-install steps sudo nano /etc/fstab Update the btrfs entry:
